skill.name: frontend-dev-guidelines
skill.description: Guidelines for building modern frontend features with Next.js, React, shadcn/ui, Tailwind CSS, TanStack Query, React Hook Form, Supabase, and TypeScript.
skill.summary: Apply consistent Next.js and React frontend patterns for production UI work.
skill.author: BOM-98

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are Markdown documentation examples, not executable skill logic. No prompt injection, malicious intent, credential exfiltration, or unsafe runtime scripts were found.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The external command detections are Markdown code fences and inline code markers used to document React, Next.js, and TypeScript patterns. They do not execute shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is inside Markdown documentation and TypeScript examples. No executable script file or command invocation is present.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Imports Are Frontend Lazy Loading Examples
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The dynamic import detections demonstrate React route and component lazy loading. They are standard frontend code-splitting examples, not obfuscated or remote code execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The imports target local modules or named libraries in instructional examples. No user-controlled import path or remote loader was found.

low_findings.title: Relative Imports Misclassified as Path Traversal
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The path traversal detections are relative import examples in frontend documentation. One cited section explicitly labels deep relative paths as an anti-pattern to avoid.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sequences occur in import paths inside Markdown examples. There is no filesystem read, write, archive extraction, or user path handling.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Lexical False Positives
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The weak cryptography alerts match ordinary words, style shorthands, or search filtering examples. No cryptographic API, hash function, or cipher implementation was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are metadata text, MUI spacing shorthand, and string matching examples. They do not contain cryptographic operations.

low_findings.title: Network Calls Are Documentation for App API Clients
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The network detections are frontend examples for using local API routes and a centralized API client. The skill does not perform outbound requests or exfiltrate data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are instructional and use same-application paths or a named project API client. No external collection endpoint or secret handling was found.

content.value_statement: Frontend teams often lose time choosing patterns for routing, data fetching, forms, and UI state. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code clear guidance for consistent React and Next.js implementation.
actual_capabilities: Guides Next.js App Router page, loading, error, layout, and API route patterns.
Explains when to use Server Components, Client Components, and Server Actions.
Provides patterns for shadcn/ui, Tailwind CSS, MUI, and reusable component organization.
Covers forms with React Hook Form and Zod validation.
Documents TanStack Query, Suspense boundaries, cache-first fetching, and mutation patterns.
Defines TypeScript conventions for props, utility types, type guards, and null handling.
limitations: It is an instructional skill, not a runnable framework or code generator package.
Some resource files include MUI and TanStack Router examples alongside the Next.js focus.
Examples must be adapted to each project structure, dependency version, and design system.
It does not replace security review, accessibility testing, or production performance profiling.

use_cases.title: Create a New Feature Page
use_cases.description: Use the skill to structure a Next.js page with loading, error, data fetching, and typed components.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team UI Patterns
use_cases.description: Use the guidelines to align component exports, file organization, form handling, and styling choices.

use_cases.title: Review React Implementation Choices
use_cases.description: Use the anti-patterns and examples to identify overused client components, weak loading states, and inconsistent imports.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Page
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a new route.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the frontend-dev-guidelines skill to create a Next.js App Router page for a product list with loading and error states.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Form Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the frontend-dev-guidelines skill to design a React Hook Form and Zod flow for editing a user profile.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor Data Fetching
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the frontend-dev-guidelines skill to refactor this component toward Suspense, TanStack Query, cache-first reads, and clear mutation handling.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Frontend Feature
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review across structure and performance.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the frontend-dev-guidelines skill to review this frontend feature for component boundaries, imports, loading states, TypeScript safety, and performance risks.

output_examples.input: Create a dashboard route that loads posts and handles failures.
output: A route plan with server-first defaults and clear component boundaries.
A loading and error state approach that matches App Router conventions.
Data fetching notes for Suspense or server-side loading based on the project context.

output_examples.input: Review this form component for guideline compliance.
output: Findings about validation, form state, submit behavior, and user feedback.
Recommended changes for React Hook Form, Zod, and reusable UI components.
Notes about any client-only code that should stay isolated.

output_examples.input: Improve performance for a data-heavy list page.
output: A focused plan covering memoization, list rendering, query cache use, and lazy loading.
Tradeoffs for Suspense boundaries and error handling.
Verification steps for bundle size, perceived loading, and interaction stability.

best_practices: Start with Server Components and add client boundaries only for hooks, browser APIs, or event handlers.
Use centralized data-fetching and API client patterns instead of one-off request logic.
Keep file organization, exports, loading states, and TypeScript types consistent across features.
anti_patterns: Adding use client to every component without a specific client-side requirement.
Mixing deep relative imports with inconsistent aliases across feature folders.
Handling loading, error, and mutation feedback differently in every component.

faq.question: Does this skill write production code automatically?
faq.answer: It guides an AI assistant toward project-ready patterns, but generated code still needs project review and testing.

faq.question: Which frontend stack does it target?
faq.answer: It mainly targets Next.js, React, shadcn/ui, Tailwind CSS, TanStack Query, React Hook Form, Zod, and TypeScript.

faq.question: Can it help with existing React code?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide refactors for component structure, data fetching, forms, imports, and loading states.

faq.question: Does it support MUI projects?
faq.answer: Some resource files include MUI examples, but the skill description is centered on Next.js and shadcn/ui.

faq.question: Does it perform network requests or run commands?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed files are Markdown guidance and examples, not executable automation scripts.

faq.question: What should users verify after applying it?
faq.answer: Users should run tests, check accessibility, confirm dependency versions, and review security-sensitive data flows.
